text:
You got it! Here’s what we know about Senate legislative efforts to improve Air Traffic Control (ATC) over the last 8 years:

One significant piece of legislation is the FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024, which was passed by the Senate in May 2024 with overwhelming bipartisan support (88-4) and was expected to pass the House and go to the President's desk.
Sponsors: This was a bipartisan effort led by Commerce Committee Chair Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.), Ranking Member Ted Cruz (R-Texas), Aviation Subcommittee Chair Tammy Duckworth (D-Ill.), and Ranking Member Jerry Moran (R-Kan.).
Intent: The bill aims to reauthorize the FAA and NTSB for five years. Its priorities include strengthening aviation safety standards, growing the air traffic controller and safety inspector workforce, boosting consumer protections, advancing technology and innovation, and modernizing the national airspace system. Specifically, it looks to increase the number of air traffic controllers, require the FAA to use new technology to prevent runway collisions, mandate 25-hour cockpit voice recorders on new planes, and make it easier for customers to get refunds for flight delays.
Fate: The bill passed the Senate on May 9, 2024. The Senate also passed a one-week extension to give the House time to pass the bill. The Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation scheduled a hearing for May 14, 2025, to review the progress and any delays in implementing this act one year later.

More recently, in the 119th Congress (2025-2026), there's the Air Traffic Control Workforce Development Act of 2025 (S.697).
Sponsor: Introduced by Senator John Hoeven (R-ND), along with Senator Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH) and Senator Jerry Moran (R-KS).
Intent: This bill seeks to address the shortage of air traffic controllers by improving training and retention. It proposes funding for tower simulator systems to reduce certification time, establishing incentive programs for trainees completing CPC qualification and for retaining current controllers, and improving mental health support for controllers. It aims to tackle issues like mandatory overtime caused by workforce shortages and the increase in near-misses at airports.
Fate: The bill was introduced on February 24, 2025, and was read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. Its current status is "Introduced," and a summary is in progress. It has not yet left committee based on the information provided.

The video you mentioned from the earlier part of the thread shows a discussion about ATC system overhauls and upgrades, including a reference to House Republicans voting against upgrades during Elaine Chao's tenure as Transportation Secretary (2017-2021). While this highlights the political dynamics around ATC funding and modernization, it doesn't point to specific Senate bills from that exact period that left committee.

So, the FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024 is a major Senate initiative that did pass the Senate and addressed ATC improvements, while S.697 is a more recent bill focused on workforce development that is currently in committee.

text:
A US government shutdown occurs when Congress fails to pass funding legislation, halting non-essential federal operations. Essential services (e.g., Social Security, air traffic control) continue, but many workers are furloughed without pay, and programs like national parks or certain agencies close.

As of Oct 1, 2025, one is underway due to disputes over a funding bill, including health care provisions. It could end once a deal is reached.

text:
A US government shutdown occurs when Congress fails to pass funding legislation, leading to a lapse in appropriations.

What happens: Non-essential federal operations halt; essential services (e.g., Social Security, air traffic control) continue. ~900K workers furloughed, ~700K work without pay. Programs like WIC may pause.

Purpose: Results from budget impasses, often over policy differences, used as negotiation leverage. In Oct 2025, disputes involve spending levels, foreign aid, and health subsidies—Republicans cite Democrat demands for immigrant healthcare; Democrats criticize Republican proposals and tactics. Ongoing as of Oct 2.

text:
Burbank Airport air traffic control tower was unmanned for hours on Monday as the U.S government shutdown triggered a shortage of air traffic controllers.

From 4:15 p.m. to 10 p.m. Monday, there were no air traffic controllers in the Burbank tower, and operations were instead run remotely by Southern California TRACON, an approach and departure team out of San Diego.

Southern California TRACON already remotely handles a lot of the air traffic in SoCal.

According to an FAA advisory, there were multiple staffing triggers in place on Monday, including delays at airports in Denver, Detroit, Indianapolis and Phoenix.

text: A US government shutdown occurs when Congress fails to pass funding legislation by the fiscal year's end, halting non-essential federal operations. Essential services like Social Security payments, military pay, and air traffic control continue, but agencies like national parks, IRS processing, and some regulatory bodies pause, furloughing hundreds of thousands of workers. NAV CANADA, which manages the country’s civil air navigation system, has conveyed airlines that air traffic control staff shortages will occur at Winnipeg and Kelowna, “but this could expand to other airports.”

Shortage of air traffic controllers in Canada is at times forcing airport towers at Kelowna and Winnipeg airports to close when the one controller on duty goes on a break.

Pilots of Air Canada have received instructions to avoid the two airports in the provinces of British Columbia and Manitoba when staffing shortages are severe enough to shut their air traffic control towers.

It was unclear how often the airports have been closed and their representatives did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

🇺🇸 RECORD-BREAKING GOVERNMENT SHUTDOWN CAUSES UNPRECEDENTED AIRPORT DELAYS

The FAA began slashing flights today across 40 major U.S. airports, launching one of the most disruptive travel weekends in recent memory.

The move is meant to “protect safety” as thousands of unpaid air traffic controllers and TSA agents reach breaking points. 

The cuts start at 4% of the flights and ramp up to 10% next week, grounding potentially thousands of flights daily.

Travelers from Houston to Honolulu are already reporting airport gridlock, missed connections, and last-minute reroutes. 

One woman even rented a U-Haul to cross the country after her flight was moved to a different city.

Airlines are scrambling to rebook, and cities like Las Vegas and Honolulu say the fallout could devastate tourism and critical services.

The skies are joining food aid, marathon routes, ambulance response, and health care on the growing list of systems buckling under this record-breaking shutdown.

Congress says it’s committed to public safety. But the planes are grounded, the sirens are quieting, and the government isn't apparently getting any closer to opening up for business again. 

Source: FOX, AP, @madeleinerivera

🚨 BREAKING: U.S. Air Travel Meltdown as Shutdown Grounds Flights Nationwide

Air travel across the United States is unraveling this weekend as the government shutdown cripples air traffic control staffing, forcing widespread delays and cancellations.

The FAA has ordered airlines to cut 4% of flights at 40 major airports—over 1,700 flights already canceled since Friday—and warns cuts could reach 10% next wee if Congress fails to end the shutdown.

With air traffic controllers and TSA agents missing paychecks, many are calling out or refusing overtime, worsening delays across the country.

American, United, Delta, and Southwest have canceled hundreds of flights and are waiving change fees as chaos spreads through New York, Atlanta, D.C., and Chicago. Some airports are reporting multi-hour waits and near-standstill security lines.

text:
🌐🇺🇲🇻🇪The US has issued a NOTAM over all of Venezuela and the southern Caribbean due to escalating tensions.

On November 21, at 17:43 UTC, the US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) issued safety NOTAM A0012/25, which covers all of Venezuela's airspace (Maiquetía FIR – SVZM) and a large part of the southern Caribbean.

The validity is set until February 19, 2026. The reason is the "deteriorating security situation and increased military activity" in the region.

The document explicitly mentions increasing GNSS (GPS) interference since September 2025 and the risks to civilian aircraft in all phases of flight and on the ground. The NOTAM does not declare a complete flight ban, but recommends "extreme caution". In practice, a number of carriers (American Airlines, Copa Airlines, LATAM) are already rerouting routes outside Venezuelan airspace, which extends flights by 30-60 minutes and increases costs.

The warning comes at a time when the US is deploying the largest number of forces in the Caribbean since the Cold War: the aircraft carrier USS Gerald R. Ford, B-52 bombers and escorting fighter jets are operating in the area.

President Trump has refused to rule out military strikes against the regime of Nicolás Maduro as part of the fight against drug trafficking. Venezuela and its allies (Russia, Cuba) have called the move a “provocation” and “preparation for aggression”. EASA and ICAO are monitoring the situation, but have not yet issued their own binding restrictions.

The NOTAM thus represents the most direct warning yet of a possible escalation in the region, which could affect civil aviation and the geopolitical stability of Latin America.

#VenezuelaNOTAM #USSouthcom #CaribbeanTensions

The Senate's move to tighten aviation safety rules after the National Airport crash is necessary, but it shouldn't take tragedy to force accountability. The Don't Cut FAA Workers Act (H.R. 2431) shows Congress finally recognizing that slashing FAA staffing after disasters compounds risk—yet this bill only addresses layoffs, not systemic failures.

Real reform requires overhauling bureaucratic inertia that allowed known safety gaps to persist. While mandating ADS-B signals helps, the real issue is Washington's reactive approach: they fund bandaids after headlines fade instead of proactive infrastructure investment.

Until we audit the FAA's $20B budget line by line, more paperwork won't prevent the next crisis.

text:
The US air traffic control shortage has become a national problem, but one former controller says the solution has been obvious for decades.

A FlightDrama reader who worked air traffic control in the United States Air Force from 1987 to 1991 says he left service fully trained and ready, only to find no clear pathway into the Federal Aviation Administration. He says the system did not know where to find qualified recruits then, and still does not.

The timing is striking. In 2025, the FAA is operating with roughly 3,000 fewer certified controllers than staffing models require, according to agency data, while delays linked to staffing constraints continue to rise across major hubs.

Former FAA administrator Randy Babbitt has said that military controllers “arrive with discipline, real-world experience, and far less training risk.” Yet there is still no direct fast-track from military ATC to FAA towers.

For some veterans, that gap meant walking away entirely. And for the system, it meant losing people who were already trained to keep the skies safe.

text:
🚨 UPDATE: Safety Regulator Mandates Fix For 586 Airbus Engines 👇

The US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has issued a critical safety order affecting major airlines flying the Airbus A320neo family. Released on January 12, 2026, "Airworthiness Directive 2025-26-04" requires urgent updates to Pratt & Whitney PW1100G engines to prevent a rare but serious fire risk.

While this legal order specifically targets 586 engines on US-registered aircraft, it is highly relevant to the global aviation community. The FAA is the primary certifying authority for these American-made engines, meaning other regulators like EASA (Europe) and DGCA (India) are most likely to follow suit with similar mandates for carriers worldwide.

The technical issue involves a domino effect. If a fan blade fractures during flight (often due to a bird strike), it can snap a critical support bracket. This structural failure puts stress on a high-pressure fuel tube, potentially causing it to crack, leak fuel, and start a fire under the engine cover.

In the US, this creates a logistical challenge for operators like Spirit Airlines, Frontier, JetBlue, Delta, United, and Hawaiian Airlines. Meanwhile, internationally, major operators of this engine type, such as Wizz Air, Lufthansa, and IndiGo, should be watching closely, as this maintenance will likely become a global standard.

Maintenance teams must remove a specific clamp within 30 days of 18th of Febuary, 2026, and eventually install redesigned brackets to eliminate the risk. This proactive move highlights why aviation remains the safest form of transport. text:
Aircraft Charter Safety
Different types of flying:
Commercial (Part 121)
Charter (Part 135)
Private (Part 91)
General Aviation (Part 91)
 
The “Part ###” refers to the type of FAA Regulations each flight operates under
 
If you do a quick look up online or ask any AI currently available they will give you some statistics based on accident rate per 100,000 flight hours
Also going to throw in One vs Two pilot crews:
Single pilot crews have a 3X higher chance of an accident and about a 10X higher chance that the accident is fatal.
As of 2020:
The Part 121 commercial airline accident rate is about .23 accidents per 100,000 flight hours
The Part 135 charter accident rate is about .7 accidents per 100,000 flight hours
The Part 91 private world 4.8 accidents per 100,000 flight hours

Then General Aviation also Part 91 was about 7.0 accidents per 100,000 flight hours
 
Okay well that’s really not too unreasonable even at the extremely “high” end of General aviation which mostly encompasses farmer Joe out for a joy ride. Remember that people do dumb stuff all the time, and your limits and rules while operating under Part 91 allow you to do much more dangerous things. So, by assuming that higher risk, you will increase your chances of an accident and decrease your safety. That’s where the Part 121 and Part 135 regulations come into play. 
 
Part 121 vs Part 135 vs Part 91:
Part 121 airlines have much more standardization across the board when it comes to operators. Pretty much everyone follows the same rules, hires the same quality of people, and has strict operating parameters and routes. Combine that with intensive training and the safety features packed onto the aircraft and you have the safest form of transportation known to main kind. Now, that doesn’t eliminate people from doing dumb things and it certainly doesn’t reduce your risk to zero, but in 2026 we have much more technology available making travel that much more safe on airlines.

Part 135 charter operations are standardized but not to the same degree as airlines. Depending on your operator you’ll find wildly different hiring requirements and quality of pilots. This is why most people stick with the big expensive names like NetJets. NJ operates much more similar to an airline than most other Part 135 operations. Now, again, it comes down to the operator and the types of pilots they hire. Passenger jets, and turbo props are smaller and typically take more risk when flying. It’s all about serving the customer and getting them from point A to Point B. The types of pilots who work in the 135 world like the challenge and sometimes added risk of challenging airports. Due to the lack of standardized airports this makes for a much more dynamic environment compared to 121. More variance as I know you understand = more risk (not just in the markets). That’s why your accident rate almost goes to 3X in this industry. I will remind you though that it is still less than 1 accident per 100,000 flight hours. (Practically zero). Part 135 regulations require more experienced pilots, FAA oversight, and limit the types of weather and operations a company can do.

Part 91 private or “fractional ownership” is pretty much the wild west of aviation. The regulations are far less limiting meaning pilots can take even more risk than the Part 135 world. Maintenance standards aren’t really required, and there’s not “standardized” training required unlike Part 121 and 135. This is really the sweet spot for private flying, BUT you really need to vet the pilots and operators to ensure you don’t end up with someone who pushing the envelope beyond what you’re comfortable with. Just because something can be done legally, doesn’t mean it’s the smartest nor is it the safest. Most private jets are operated under Part 91, but depending on who runs the business drastically changes the safety. Most of the private jets you see crash are operated under part 91, and normally they crash due to someone cutting corners somewhere. Again legal, but not necessarily safe.
Who do you choose and why?
Most of your “charter” flights should be done under part 135 its up to you to find an operator you trust. Safety ratings and certifications do help, but they don’t show the whole picture. If you can get in touch with the owner of the operation that will tell you a ton about the type of business he/she runs. 
 
What aircraft?
Turbo prop (PC12, King Air, Piagio, TBM, Piper M600)

- You use these for trips that are about 1,000 miles. They stay low and while they can hit decently high speeds, that’s not the main benefit. They are robust and have great performance to price ratios. They won’t typically fly as high as jets, but that’s what makes them ideal for more regional trips
Light Jet (Cessna Citation M2, Phenom 300, Honda Jet)
- Typically you aren’t going to take more than 2-4 people on these and they only require 1 pilot. Now in my original reply you’ll remember 2 pilots is always safer than one. You can request an extra pilot if they don’t already require it but that will limit what/who you can take with you
Midsized Jets (Cessna Citation XLS, etc)
- You start to see two pilot crews required here (still capable of single pilot ops) but most places require two pilots. Also the experience required to fly one of these increases further making them safer than the light jets and turbo props in some cases. Again, it all comes down to the operator and types of pilots that they hire to crew the planes. These planes will also get you from point A to point B quicker in more comfort and increase your costs.
Super Midsized jets (Challenger 350)
- Two pilot crews required, typically 5-10 years of experience in the jet world to pilot one. Cost is much higher, but so are the capabilities and you can take 8+ of your friends. As far as a safety thing if you have the money, this is one of your best bets.

Heavy jets (Gulfstream G550, Challenger 650)
- Safety is basically negligible at this point compared to the super midsized. Pilots are extremely well trained and typically much more senior in their careers, but that doesn’t make them too much safer compared to the super mids.

Flying private comes down to a lot of things:
Budget
# of people

Speed
Comfort
Distance you need to travel
What you want onboard

I fly professionally and am dipping my toes into the turbine world more and more every year. I don’t worry about most things when flying because I know my training and I trust myself. That’s a similar mindset you’ll find in most pilots, but not all are as safety conscious. We are all mission and goal focused and I think that sometimes that gets the better of us and we make stupid decisions. That’s why having a hard limit set by the Part 121/135 world helps improve their safety records.
So if I was vetting a company for their safety mindset here’s what I would ask them: 
“What are your accident/incident reporting procedures?”
“Do you have an inhouse safety rep/expert?”
“Do you have a company ASRS program (aviation safety reporting system)?”
“How often do your pilots go to recurrent training?”
“What are the minimum hours and background experience to be hired here?”
“Who does your maintenance? And who supervises them?”
“What are your weather minimum op specs?”
 
Hope this helps you out a bit more than my last post @BowTiedIguana

text:
The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has issued a series of critical safety advisories (NOTAMs) warning commercial airlines to exercise caution when flying over Mexico, Central America, and parts of South America.

The advisories cover a broad geographical area and are scheduled to remain in effect for 60 days, through March 17, 2026.

Affected Regions: Space over Mexico, Panama, Colombia, Ecuador, and the Mazatlán Oceanic Flight Region.

Offshore Areas: Significant portions of the Eastern Pacific Ocean and the Gulf of California.

Altitude: The risks apply to aircraft at all altitudes, including those in overflight, arrival, and departure phases.
The warnings come amid an escalation of U.S. military presence and rhetoric in Latin America. President Trump has recently suggested the possibility of U.S. military strikes against "drug cartel" land targets within Mexico.

Safety concerns were heightened by a recent "near miss" where a JetBlue passenger jet had to take evasive action to avoid a U.S. Air Force tanker flying without a transponder near the Venezuelan coast.

By modernizing its technical operations and prioritizing world-class education for its team, Frontier continues to set a high bar for maintenance excellence in the aviation industry.

text:
Illinois Senators Push FAA to Prioritize ORD for Staffing and Modernization

Illinois Senators Dick Durbin and Tammy Duckworth are urging the FAA to make Chicago O’Hare International Airport a top priority for staffing, technology upgrades, and infrastructure investment. In a letter to FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford, the senators cited ORD’s status as the nation’s busiest airport in 2025—with more than 857,000 annual operations—as proof of its critical role in the national aviation system. 

They’re calling on the FAA to address air traffic controller shortages, modernize systems, and fully utilize all available runways, including Runway 9R, to reduce delays and improve efficiency. The senators also encouraged the agency to give strong consideration to federal funding for ORD’s Terminal Area Plan, a modernization project that would expand gate capacity by about 25%. These investments, they say, are vital to maintaining safety, efficiency, and reliability for travelers across the country.

Read the full letter and more, click here: https://t.co/aYQJKjiziu

text:
🚦 Runway Status Lights (RWSL) — FAA’s silent safety guardian
#AC8646 #LaGuardia 

RWSL is an automated, surveillance-driven system—no controller input—using radar to detect conflicts and warn pilots in real time.

🔴 REL (Runway Entrance Lights)
RED → Do NOT enter (traffic on/approaching runway)

My recent article and blog highlight airport signage: shapes and colour. https://t.co/Gg8RJB5EGg
⚠️ Human Factors — Purkinje Effect
At night, the eye shifts sensitivity → green appears brighter than red
➡️ Green taxi/centreline lights may dominate visually
➡️ Critical RED RWSL cues can be under-perceived

Key point:
➡️ RWSL works independent of ATC clearance
➡️ It is a last-line safety barrier
➡️ RED always overrides clearance

At complex layouts (e.g. LaGuardia), multiple REL/THL layers protect intersecting traffic  

Bottom line:
Clearance ≠ Safety
If it’s RED → STOP.

#AviationSafety #HumanFactors #RWSL #FAA #RunwayIncursion 
@NTSB @JenniferHomendy @FAANews @icao

text:
There is, so far, little clarity about the occupants of the firetruck involved in the recent plane crash.

In the absence of clear information, explanations quickly emerge.

Some point to understaffed air traffic control and aging infrastructure.

From there, the argument expands: insufficient public investment, budget constraints, and ultimately taxation policy.

We are told that governments spend poorly, that taxes are too high—particularly for the wealthy—and that this constrains efficiency.

But this explanation only touches the surface.

A deeper structural question appears when we look at how wealth actually circulates.

Global billionaire wealth is estimated in the tens of trillions of dollars.

At the same time, large portions of this wealth are not held as simple income, but as financial assets and debt structures, which are often taxed differently—or not at all in practical terms.

Meanwhile, in the United States:
- Total GDP is on the order of ~$30 trillion annually
- A significant share of economic activity is tied to financial markets rather than direct production

This raises a key question:
How much of economic activity is generating real goods and services—and how much is circulating within financial systems themselves?

To understand the imbalance, imagine an economy as a flowing river.
If a growing portion of that flow is diverted into large, accumulating reservoirs—financial holdings that are rarely reintegrated into everyday economic life—then circulation slows where it matters most:
- wages
- infrastructure
- education
- public systems
- human development

The issue is not simply that wealth exists at the top.
It is that its circulation becomes structurally limited.

When capital concentrates without returning proportionally to the broader economy, it begins to shape more than markets:
- It shapes narratives.
- It defines what is considered “efficient,” what is “realistic,” and what is “unavoidable.”

Over time, structural issues—like underinvestment in critical systems—can be explained away through simplified stories, while their deeper causes remain largely unexamined.

At that point, the problem is no longer only economic.
It becomes epistemological—a question of how reality itself is understood.

If systems are analyzed only through measurable outputs, deficits, and costs, then the human dimension—well-being, coherence, long-term health—tends to disappear from view.

We become highly precise in measuring breakdown,
but less capable of recognizing what a healthy system would actually look like.
The result is a society that functions—sometimes impressively—
yet struggles to perceive its own imbalances.

Not because they are hidden,
but because the framework used to interpret them is incomplete.

text:
EVERY crisis can be traced back to a democrat. 

"The FAA Staffing Shortage Biden Left Behind Killed Two Pilots

Here's what the media won't tell you about what happened at LaGuardia Sunday night.

The FAA's air traffic controller workforce fell by nearly 2,000 employees between 2010 and 2024 – a 13 percent reduction. The Biden years were supposed to reverse that. They didn't. The FAA's own workforce plans show it was hitting constrained hiring targets that aviation safety experts said were far too low to meet actual system needs. The National Academy of Sciences concluded the FAA needed to maintain more than 14,000 controllers to handle demand. Biden's FAA was targeting 12,000 while actually running closer to 10,600.

The Senate's FAA reauthorization bill – the legislation that was supposed to fix controller staffing – spent nearly a year stalled under Democratic leadership. Senator Michael Bennet held the entire bill hostage to leverage a separate immigration deal. The FAA's authorization expired and had to be extended on stopgap after stopgap while pilots and controllers kept working in a system nobody was funding properly.

Biden also went 18 months without a confirmed FAA administrator while the staffing crisis was building. The agency drifted leaderless through near-miss after near-miss.

The result: LaGuardia, one of the most technically demanding airports in America – two intersecting runways packed into a tight Queens footprint with almost no margin for error – was four controllers short of its own target. And that was considered well-staffed compared to the rest of the system.

Former DOT Inspector General Mary Schiavo was direct about Sunday night: "Giving a fire truck clearance to cross the runway after an aircraft has been cleared to run in on final is a clear error. There's just no way around that."

Retired air traffic controller Harvey Sconick, who spent 38 years with the FAA, was more blunt: "The controller who crossed those vehicles while the airplane was landing just went brain-dead for a minute."

Controllers don't go brain-dead in a vacuum. They go brain-dead when they're managing two emergencies simultaneously on an understaffed overnight shift after years of forced overtime and back-to-back workdays – a direct consequence of the Biden FAA's failure to build the workforce the system needed.

Sean Duffy Shows Up at LaGuardia While Democrats Dodge the FAA Mess They Made

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy was on site at LaGuardia Monday morning. He called the crash "horrific." He walked straight into the cameras and told the truth about what he inherited.

Duffy has been fighting to fix this since day one. He launched a plan to supercharge the controller workforce with financial incentives, expanded military hiring pipelines, and accelerated training resources. He called for additional funding Monday standing in front of the wreckage: "I've been asking Congress for additional money. We are modernizing our system, but we can't fully modernize it until Congress gives us additional money."

He's right. LaGuardia already has an advanced surface surveillance system – the alarm from that system is audible in the tower audio from Sunday night. The technology flagged the conflict in real time. A human being cleared the truck anyway – a human being stretched thin by years of understaffing that Biden and Senate Democrats allowed to fester.

The ALERT Act and the ROTOR Act – aviation safety bills responding directly to the 67 deaths over the Potomac – are still moving through Congress. Fourteen months of hearings, promises, and press releases while Duffy tries to rebuild a system Democrats ran into the ground.

The conditions that killed those two pilots were not a surprise. The runway incursion history at LaGuardia was documented. The understaffing was documented. The pattern of overwhelmed controllers managing simultaneous emergencies on skeleton overnight crews was documented. The FAA's own safety review team called the staffing situation an "incident precursor" as far back as 2023.

Biden read those reports. His FAA leadership read those reports. Senate Democrats held the reauthorization bill hostage for nearly a year rather than funding the fixes.

Two young men who dedicated their careers to flying passengers safely are dead tonight.

Sean Duffy is doing his job. He was at the wreckage. He is demanding the funding. He is rebuilding a system his predecessor left broken.

The question is whether the same Democrats who spent four years underfunding the FAA will spend the next four years blocking the solutions – or whether two more dead pilots is finally enough to make them move."
-BlueStatesBluesNews

text:
Federal investigators: One of only two air traffic controllers on duty at LaGuardia Airport cleared a fire truck to cross a runway just 12 seconds before an Air Canada flight touched down, leaving little time to avoid the collision that killed both pilots.

National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) confirms two air traffic controllers were on duty in the LaGuardia tower during the Air Canada runway collision with a fire truck (killing 2 pilots). It's standard midnight-shift procedure for two to handle multiple roles in that busy airspace.

In a somber exchange following the impact, the controller admitted to a colleague that he had been "dealing with an emergency earlier" and confessed, "I messed up."

The NTSB also confirms 25 hours of audio suggests a modern CVR system, as newer aircraft have been upgraded from the older 2-hour loop to much longer recording durations.

text:
SAFETY QUESTIONS MOUNT OVER LAGUARDIA COLLISION 🚨

Protocol Breach: Despite being involved in the fatal collision between a plane and a fire engine, the air traffic controller reportedly remained on duty, raising serious questions about FAA safety protocols.

text:
Visuals: Pilots carried the casket of Jazz Aviation first officer Mackenzie Gunther off a plane at the Ottawa International Airport on Thursday afternoon.

The bodies of the two Air Canada Express pilots killed in a collision at New York’s LaGuardia Airport on Sunday have been returned to Canada.

Capt. Antoine Forest's body was then flown from Ottawa to Montréal Trudeau International Airport.

Hundreds of pilots and flight staff waited in the rain, lined up in front of Air Canada’s Montreal headquarters to honour Forest.

Captain Tim Perry, president of the Air Line Pilots Association Canada: “No family should go through this,” he said. “It must be the promise that when a pilot leaves to work or when anyone leaves to travel by air that they come home alive. Our work begins tomorrow to live up to that promise.”

Transportation Safety Board of Canada and the U.S. National Transportation Safety Board continue to examine the circumstances that led allowing both the incoming aircraft and the fire truck were cleared onto the same runway.

text:
The CEO of Air Canada will retire later this year, after being criticised for failing to speak French in a condolence video following last week's fatal collision at LaGuardia Airport that killed two of the airline's pilots.

Michael Rousseau informed the airline that he will be stepping down by the end of the company's third quarter, Air Canada said in a statement on Monday.

Rousseau had faced calls to resign after delivering his condolences in English only. One of the pilots who died, Antoine Forest, was from French-speaking Quebec.

He later apologised and said he was unable to express himself "adequately" in French - an official language in Canada - despite taking lessons over the years.

Two Air Canada pilots just died in a horrific LaGuardia runway crash in New York—and instead of focusing on the tragedy, grieving families, or airline safety—Prime Minister Mark Carney is out here publicly SHAMING the CEO for posting an ENGLISH-ONLY condolence video?!

He actually said it showed “a lack of judgment and a lack of compassion” because it wasn’t bilingual. While the house is literally on fire (economy tanking, real crises everywhere), Carney’s wasting oxygen chastising a corporate exec like he’s some language hall monitor.

A CORPORATION IS NOT BILINGUAL. It’s a LEGAL FRAMEWORK!

It has ZERO responsibility to speak multiple languages!!

The CEO expressed his sadness in his mother tongue—and he was 100% right to do it. The message is the message.
Meanwhile, watching Carney fumble through French is straight-up embarrassing—zero enunciation, horrific timing. Just STOP with the forced dual-language pandering already! It’s nothing but virtue-signaling to a minority province that doesn’t even honor English in its own borders (looking at you, Quebec and Bill 101).

This is textbook MICRO-MANAGEMENT and government overreach. Mark Carney is now inserting himself into private corporate protocol and setting a dangerous precedent.

Sound like anyone you know? Xi Jinping and Vladimir Putin come to mind real quick.
Priorities, people! When did we let language police run the country?

Michael Rousseau did what any sane executive would do: deliver a clear, compassionate message in English, the language of international aviation and the overwhelming majority of Air Canada’s customers and employees. Rousseau is retiring. Mission accomplished for the virtue-signaling machine.

StatsCan just reported 84,000 net job losses in February 2026 alone. Unemployment is at 6.7%.

text:
Post 1. 

The True Cost of Canada’s Bilingual Obsession  

31st March 2026  

In late March 2026, two Air Canada Express pilots, Antoine Forest, a francophone from Quebec, and Mackenzie Gunther from Ontario, perished in a runway collision at New York’s LaGuardia Airport aboard Flight 8646. The loss was devastating, a stark reminder of the human stakes in aviation safety. Families mourned, colleagues grieved, and Canadians from coast to coast should have come together in solemn reflection.  

Instead, the tragedy was swiftly hijacked by the politics of language. Air Canada chief executive Michael Rousseau issued a four-minute video of condolences, beginning with the words “bonjour” and “merci,” accompanied by full French subtitles. The message itself was delivered in English, the language of international aviation under International Civil Aviation Organisation standards and the mother tongue of roughly three quarters of Canadians.  

Quebec Premier François Legault demanded Rousseau’s resignation. The Office of the Commissioner of Official Languages logged hundreds of complaints. Prime Minister Mark Carney publicly rebuked the chief executive for “lack of compassion” and “poor judgment.” Within days Rousseau had apologised and announced his retirement.  

This episode, now known as the Air Canada affair, is far more than a corporate communications misstep. It lays bare the corrosive price of Ottawa’s relentless pandering to bilingualism, a policy that dishonours the dead, elevates mediocrity above merit, and quietly nurtures a double standard of cultural protectionism that the Liberal government condemns everywhere except in Quebec.  

At its core, the affair exposes a deeper Canadian reality: a federation where language has become a tool of political control rather than a bridge of understanding, where identity optics routinely trump measurable outcomes, and where citizens are asked to accept unequal rules in the name of unity.  

Canada’s federal Official Languages Act, modernised under Justin Trudeau and faithfully upheld under Mark Carney, binds institutions such as Air Canada, which was privatised with explicit bilingual obligations. The result is a system that demands French alongside English in public communications, hiring, and leadership, regardless of operational necessity.  

Quebec, by contrast, operates under its own uncompromising framework. Bill 101, the 1977 Charter of the French Language, and its successor Bill 96, which took full effect in 2025, declare French the sole official language of work, commerce, education, and signage. French must be “markedly predominant,” appearing at least twice the size of any other language. Businesses with twenty five or more employees face mandatory francisation audits. Immigrants are funnelled into French schools after six months. The Office québécois de la langue française polices compliance with fines and enforcement teams.  

These laws are shielded by the notwithstanding clause, overriding Charter equality rights and creating, in effect, two tiers of Canadian citizenship.  

The immediate consequence of such asymmetry was visible in the Air Canada affair. A competent chief executive, who had spent fourteen years based in Montreal and invested hundreds of hours in French lessons, was hounded from his post not for operational failure but for delivering condolences primarily in English.  

The pilots’ deaths, one francophone and one anglophone, became secondary to whether subtitles and greetings satisfied Quebec’s expectations of linguistic primacy. Safety briefings, international coordination, and factual updates, all conducted in the global standard of English, were politicised within hours. Families watching their private loss turned into partisan theatre deserved better. The dead were dishonoured by a rush to score points rather than honour lives.

text:
The new Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) restrictions prohibit flights from making side-by-side approaches to SFO’s parallel east-west runways in clear weather, even when the pilots acknowledge having the other aircraft in sights.

The FAA on Tuesday imposed new safety restrictions on landings at San Francisco International Airport as the airport begins a major runway project, a combination expected to reduce arrivals and cause delays for travelers over the coming months.

“San Francisco International Airport (SFO) will experience some flight delays due to a runway repaving project and an FAA safety measure.”

The agency added in the statement that its safety measure bars landing pathways for planes in which they are “side-by-side” and aims for the airport’s “parallel east-west runways in clear weather when the pilots acknowledge having the other aircraft in sight”

“It requires staggered approaches, with one aircraft offset from the aircraft on the parallel runway. The FAA never allowed side-by-side approaches in bad weather,” the FAA said.

🚨 PILOTS ALMOST LANDED AT LAGUARDIA WITHOUT PERMISSION WHILE TALKING TO THE WRONG CONTROL TOWER — DAYS AFTER DEADLY CRASH

A Delta Connection flight approaching LaGuardia nearly landed without proper clearance after contacting the wrong air traffic control tower.

The aircraft was about 2 miles from landing when the crew requested clearance… but they were communicating with JFK Tower instead of LaGuardia.

• Incorrect frequency selected
• Aircraft already on final approach
• JFK controller responds, confused

At one point, another pilot can be heard on the frequency saying: 
“That’s crazy.”

The controller then asks the crew to confirm their location before instructing them to switch to LaGuardia Tower.

The pilots executed a go-around and later landed safely.

The FAA says it is investigating the incident.

This comes just days after a fatal runway collision at LaGuardia that killed two pilots.

Both incidents occurred at the same airport within days.

Why are there so many aviation incidents happening all at once?

text:
FAA Restrictions and Construction to Disrupt SFO

SFO has commenced a six-month repaving project on Runway 1R/19L & 1L/19R and nearby taxiways, a critical infrastructure upgrade designed to improve safety and taxiway efficiency.

Additionally, the FAA has placed restrictions on its close parallel visual approaches to it’s east-west runways, known as the TIPP TOE and BRIDGE visual approaches to 28L and 28R, respectively. 

Yesterday the FAA released this statement, “San Francisco International Airport (SFO) will experience some flight delays due to a runway repaving project and an FAA safety measure. The airport’s runway project will put the two north-south runways out of service for approximately six months. The FAA safety measure prohibits flights from making side-by-side approaches to SFO’s parallel east-west runways in clear weather when the pilots acknowledge having the other aircraft in sight. It requires staggered approaches, with one aircraft offset from the aircraft on the parallel runway. The FAA never allowed side-by-side approaches in bad weather. The FAA is exploring ways to safely increase the airport arrival rate.”
Source: FAA
Read the full story: https://t.co/FDVijdtVAb

Yes, this list from pilots is largely accurate based on FAA, airline data, and safety studies.

2. True—rear seats (esp. middle) show highest survival rates in crash data. 
3. True—HEPA filters + frequent air changes make cabin air cleaner than most offices. 
4. True—airlines require pilots to eat different meals (company policy, often in ops manuals). 
5. True—flights carry contingency + reserve fuel; shortages aren't a real risk. 
6. True—autopilot handles 90%+ of flight time; pilots monitor constantly. 
7. True—planes get struck ~1x/year and are engineered for it (Faraday cage effect). 
8. True—brace position reduces head/spine injuries (FAA & crash studies confirm). 
9. True—airplane mode is for ground network congestion, not plane navigation. 
10. True per some stats—dawn/dusk have fewer weather/traffic issues. 
11. True—dimmed lights aid eye adjustment for night evacuations.

Fly safe! ✈️

text:
Correct.  

My brother-in-law is an FAA safety inspector.

If there is even a "near miss" they do a thorough investigation and come up with systems to prevent it from happening again.  

Even if it was the fault of an individual, the answer isn't "Tell them to do better".  It is "How we build a system around this so that even if the human makes that mistake it gets caught."

And it's not that nobody can get in trouble.  Say a pilot is drunk while flying - yes, they'll get in trouble.

But in medicine, we never admit near misses.  Even when there's actual harm, we are taught to just keep quiet and hope that they patient doesn't realize it was our fault.  Good doctors don't do that, they reach out and apologize, but we are taught to not do that.  

The best answer I've ever been able to come up with that could actually work is to remove personal liability of physicians.  Make it so only the hospital or medical practice can get sued.

That way the big entities with deep pockets have incentive to put systems in place to help the providers.

Right now the incentives for the hospitals and practices is to put as little as possible into helping the provider avoid mistakes because anything they do just adds to overhead and if something bad does happen they don't care - it is personal liability for the physician, primarily.

There is certainly some liability for the hospital or practice employing the physician, but it's mainly the phyuician who bears the responsibility personally.

text:
ATSTC INAUGURATES AIR TRAFFIC CONTROL AND SURVEILLANCE RADAR COURSE 3, STRENGTHENS NATIONAL AIRSPACE SAFETY AND SECURITY

The Air Traffic Services Training Centre (ATSTC), Kaduna, on Tuesday, 7 April 2026, inaugurated the Air Traffic Control and Surveillance Radar Course 3 (ACSR 3) for 10 Nigerian Air Force Officers of the Air Traffic Services specialty and 10 Nigerian Airspace Management Agency (NAMA) ATCO cadets. Coming barely two months after the inauguration of ACSR 2 for 20 NAMA cadets, the new course underscores the Centre’s sustained commitment to closing the backlog of Air Traffic Control Officer trainees while strengthening Nigeria’s air navigation manpower for both civil and military aviation operations. The joint training initiative further reinforces the longstanding civil-military collaboration between the NAF and NAMA, a partnership critical to enhancing the safety, efficiency and expeditious coordination of air operations across the Nigerian airspace in support of national development and security.

The inauguration ceremony was attended by the representative of the Airspace Manager, Alhaji Abdulmajid Muhammed, who also serves as the Air Traffic Operations Manager at Hassan Usman Katsina International Airport, alongside the Acting Deputy Commandant, distinguished instructors, principal staff officers and deans of the various schools at the Centre. Presiding over the event, the Commandant of ATSTC, Air Vice Marshal NB Folaji, reaffirmed the Centre’s dedication to strategic capacity building and seamless collaboration with NAMA in developing the next generation of highly skilled air traffic controllers. He charged the participants to remain adaptable, disciplined, resourceful and team-oriented throughout the 65-week programme, stressing that effective time management, confidence, sound work ethics and personal discipline would be vital to success.

Air Vice Marshal Folaji further emphasized that the course is structured to deliver internationally benchmarked professional competence in air traffic control and surveillance radar operations, in line with global aviation best practices. He noted that ATSTC has continued to strengthen its reputation as a credible centre of excellence for the training of airspace management professionals whose expertise remains indispensable to mission planning, airspace deconfliction, flight safety and seamless coordination of military air operations. By sustaining this high standard of training and deepening collaboration with critical civil aviation stakeholders, the Nigerian Air Force continues to reinforce the operational backbone required for effective air power projection, national airspace security and the advancement of Nigeria’s aviation ecosystem.

#NigerianAirForce #ATSTC #AirTrafficControl #AirspaceSafety #NationalSecurity #CapacityBuilding #CivilMilitaryCooperation #AviationDevelopment #NAF #NAMA

The Brazilian Anac (National Civil Aviation Agency) is studying the expansion of operating hours at Congonhas Airport following the air traffic control shutdown that affected flights in São Paulo this Thursday (9).

The interruption occurred after the evacuation of a building housing a Decea (Department of Airspace Control) sector of the FAB (Brazilian Air Force), responsible for managing air traffic, called the Terminal Control Area, or TMA.

A technical problem in the Approach Control (APP) at Congonhas and Guarulhos airports in São Paulo caused delays in 10 flights with landings and takeoffs at Marechal Rondon International Airport this Thursday.

The situation is a reflection of a technical problem that affected the control center in São Paulo, causing a cascading effect across the country.

🎥 MidiaJur

text:
Here’s why “gamifying” the job (smartly) could actually help:  

Air traffic control is one of the most stressful jobs on earth. Controllers juggle dozens of planes at once, lives on the line, old-school tools, and constant pressure. New hires are flooding in… but many burn out or wash out because the mental load is brutal.  

Think about why millions stay glued to video games for hours: the challenge feels “just right,” you get instant feedback, and it keeps you focused without frying your brain.  

That’s not turning ATC into a video game. It’s borrowing proven tricks—cleaner screens, smart alerts, balanced workload—so controllers stay sharp, calm, and in the zone longer.  

Hire a few experts who know ATC systems + game design + how the human brain works. Gently layer it onto the old tech. Result? Fewer mistakes, happier teams, safer skies, and the new generation actually sticks around.  

Future of aviation isn’t just more people. It’s better systems that work with us, not against us.

text:
If radar systems fail to function properly, air traffic controllers lose real-time tracking of aircraft positions, speeds, and altitudes. 

Likely outcomes include:
- Shift to procedural control (relying on pilot position reports), requiring larger separation distances between planes—reducing airspace capacity and causing flight delays or cancellations.
- Elevated collision risks, especially in busy corridors.
- Potential grounding of flights, route diversions, or restrictions to maintain safety.
- Economic hits: lost overflight revenue for Nigeria, airline rerouting, and possible ICAO safety downgrades affecting international ops.

Upgrades or backups like ADS-B would be critical to mitigate.

text:
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and Department of War (DOW) completed a thorough safety assessment of a high-energy laser counter-drone system. This assessment validated that proper safety controls are in place and that while these systems are at the cutting edge of counter drone technology, they do not pose undue risk to passenger aircraft.

The FAA and the DOW completed the safety assessment after observing demonstrations of the system last month. This Administration upholds the highest standard for safeguarding Americans and will build-in this methodology from the start for systems employed in the U.S. Homeland.

"The FAA's top priority is protecting the safety of the American flying public, and we value the collaboration with the Department of War in that effort," said FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford. "Following a thorough, data-informed Safety Risk Assessment, we determined that these systems do not present an increased risk to the flying public. We will continue working with our interagency partners to ensure the National Airspace System remains safe while addressing emerging drone threats."

"This successful test showcases the significant advancements we're making in counter-drone technology to ensure that our warfighters have the most advanced tools to defend the homeland," said U.S. Army Brigadier General Matt Ross, director of JIATF-401. "By working hand-in-hand with the FAA and our interagency partners, the Department of War is proving that these cutting-edge capabilities are safe, effective, and ready to protect all air travelers from illicit drone use in the national airspace."

The DOW will continue to coordinate with the FAA to safely employ this system and ensure civilian aircraft, pilots, navigation equipment, or air traffic services are not impacted.

text:
🚨 TECH: FAA LAUNCHES GAMER RECRUITMENT DRIVE TO FIX DECADES-LONG AIR TRAFFIC CONTROLLER SHORTAGE

The @FAANews is launching a recruitment campaign specifically targeting video gamers to fill thousands of vacant air traffic controller positions. The application window opens April 17 and will close as soon as the FAA receives 8,000 applications, which officials expect to hit within days. The YouTube ad opens with a montage of Fortnite and Madden gameplay, asking "Are you up for the challenge?" and promising an average $158,000 salary after three years.

The FAA currently has 11,000 controllers in service and 4,000 trainees in the pipeline, but remains roughly 3,500 short of the 14,600 needed.  Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said the gaming focus "taps into a growing demographic of young adults who have many of the hard skills it takes to be a successful controller." 

This is the FAA's second attempt at gamer-focused recruitment after a similar 2021 campaign. The agency has gamified the application process itself, telling recruits to "level up your career" and comparing the application to a "quest."

text:
History of aviation safety regs: 1926 Air Commerce Act imposed standards at industry urging to enable commercial growth amid high crash rates. 1938 CAA/1940 CAB added economic oversight + safety rules. FAA created 1958 after mid-air collisions. 

Accidents repeatedly drove upgrades (e.g., post-1950s jet fatigue rules, 1970s hijacking protocols). 1978 deregulation cut fares/competition but kept strict safety enforcement; fatal accident rate fell ~80% since 1990s as passenger volume tripled.

Boeing 737 MAX: profit-driven MCAS shortcut (avoided $10B retraining) hid risks, caused 2018-19 crashes killing 346. FAA grounded fleet 20+ months, mandated redesigns costing billions—enforcement beyond voluntary thresholds. 

Data: regs set floors where pure market incentives fell short.

text:
FAA Targets Gamers to Close Air Traffic Controller Shortage

The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the U.S. Department of Transportation are turning to gamers in an effort to address the shortage of air traffic controllers in the United States.

Applications are set to open on April 17, with the goal of reducing staffing gaps across the sector. The FAA currently employs around 11,000 active controllers, with approximately 4,000 trainees in the pipeline. However, thousands more are needed for the system to reach full capacity.

U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy stated that new communication strategies are required to reach younger generations, noting that this gaming-focused approach targets individuals with skills such as rapid decision-making, focus, and the ability to manage complex processes.

The program offers paid training, federal employment benefits, and an average annual salary exceeding $155,000 after three years.

The FAA previously launched a similar campaign targeting gamers in 2021. According to Department of Transportation data, only about 25% of current controllers hold a traditional university degree, while roughly 65% of the U.S. population regularly plays video games. The agency believes that the cognitive skills developed through gaming can provide an advantage in high-intensity roles such as air traffic control.

text:
The U.S. government can now use a counterdrone laser along the southern border after the Pentagon and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) agreed the weapons are safe, the two agencies announced Friday.

The Defense Department and FAA “completed a thorough safety assessment of a high-energy laser counter-drone system” in New Mexico last month and agreed the laser does not pose undue risks to passenger aircraft.

The agreement signed by the two agencies lays out the safety precautions in effect when these lasers are used to protect airline travelers. It comes after the FAA on two occasions was forced to close parts of Texas airspace after antidrone lasers were deployed.

The El Paso airport was forced to close for several hours in February after the FAA temporarily closed the airspace over the city. The FAA initially announced a 10-day closure of the airspace but lifted the restrictions after less than seven hours.

FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford made the decision to close the airspace without notifying the White House, Department of Homeland Security or the Pentagon.

text:
FAA and Pentagon officials now say the high‑energy counter‑drone laser that triggered February’s El Paso airport shutdown is safe for use along the southern border, following a joint safety assessment that concluded it does not pose undue risk to passenger aircraft.

🔧 What changed after the shutdown:
- A full safety review in New Mexico validated that the laser system’s controls and operating procedures meet FAA risk‑mitigation standards.   
- A formal interagency agreement now governs when and how the system can be activated, including required precautions to avoid interference with civilian aviation.   
- New pilot advisories instruct aircraft near the U.S.–Mexico border to use ADS‑B OUT within five nautical miles or risk being affected by counter‑drone operations. 

🛑 Why El Paso shut down in February:
- The FAA abruptly halted all flights after a Homeland Security unit used the Pentagon’s laser system before the FAA completed its safety review. The agency initially announced a 10‑day closure but lifted it after roughly eight hours.   
- Conflicting explanations followed: officials first blamed a suspected cartel drone incursion, but multiple reports later showed the trigger was Pentagon testing on a party balloon misidentified as a drone.

text:
AIRCRAFT CP-3243 GOES MISSING AFTER MID-AIR EMERGENCY IN BOLIVIA

An aircraft registered CP-3243 has reportedly crashed after losing contact with air traffic control shortly after its departure from La Paz, Bolivia, earlier today.

According to reports, the aircraft entered a prolonged circling pattern approximately 24 minutes after takeoff, remaining airborne for nearly 2 hours before eventually losing altitude. The last signal was recorded at 14:57:57, as tracked by Flightradar24.

Authorities say the crew had declared a state of emergency, with early indications pointing to possible cabin depressurization or a lack of oxygen on board.

In response, a K-8 aircraft from the Bolivian Air Force (FAB) was deployed over Cochabamba to help identify the aircraft’s last known coordinates after it began flying an unusual route.

Search and investigation efforts are currently underway.

text:
Cessna Citation CP-3243. 
Flight data suggests potential "ghost flight" in Bolivia.

An aircraft with registration CP-3243, which took off at 8:19 local time in Bolivia (12:19 UTC) from El Alto Airport in La Paz, ceased contact with air traffic control around 8:50. Its destination was El Trompillo Airport in Santa Cruz de la Sierra.

The aircraft, a Cessna Citation, entered a circular pattern starting at 8:55 that was repeated about 20 times while descending from 39,000 feet until its signal was lost at 19,375 feet at 10:57 local time, as can be observed on FlightRadar24.

This flight pattern is characteristic of what in aviation is called a "ghost flight," a situation where automatic navigation systems maintain the aircraft's trajectory while the occupants are unconscious, likely due to decompression or a failure in the pressurization system.

The situation of CP-3243 bears a parallel with the accident of golfer Payne Stewart in 1999, when his aircraft, a Learjet 35, flew aimlessly for four hours over U.S. territory following a sudden depressurization. Similarly, history records similar events such as Helios Airways Flight 522 in 2005, which orbited over Athens automatically for more than an hour, cases in which fuel exhaustion was the only factor that ended the trajectory of aircraft manned by already incapacitated people.

In any case, the results of official investigations must be awaited.

Local media released a photograph of what is possibly the crashed aircraft.
https://t.co/g5pmuwvLVD
